title: Disease Severity: Conceptualization & Computation
description: My MS thesis is building a unified conceptual and computational framework for disease severity — a construct that is everywhere in clinical medicine but rarely defined rigorously. I'm conducting a PRISMA-ScR scoping review to synthesize how severity is conceptualized across clinical domains, then formalizing that into a multilevel, multidimensional model that can represent dynamic severity trajectories across disease stages and patient contexts.
whyItMatters: Every clinical decision — treatment intensity, escalation, prognosis — implicitly involves severity. Yet severity scores like APACHE-II or SOFA are predictive instruments that have been reified into measures of disease state. Without a clear conceptual foundation, computational representations of severity encode hidden assumptions that downstream models inherit uncritically.

title: Real-World Evidence & EHR Data Infrastructure
description: I'm building expertise in the full stack of real-world evidence research — from processing multi-state administrative claims databases (HCUP, 48M+ records) to developing computable phenotypes and cohort discovery pipelines in OMOP CDM using OHDSI ATLAS. I'm interested in how choices made early in data preparation — cohort definitions, ICD filtering, claims-EHR concordance — propagate into downstream conclusions.
whyItMatters: I've studied diagnostic concordance between administrative claims and EHR systems across 17M patients at 56 healthcare organizations (JAMIA Open, in-press). The gap is substantial and systematic. RWE can only be trusted when the data infrastructure it rests on is understood — and that understanding requires careful empirical work, not just methods papers.

title: Clinical NLP & LLM Applications
description: I'm exploring how NLP and large language models can extract clinically actionable structure from unstructured healthcare text — at scale and reliably. My current focus is AgentDose: a framework combining rule-based NLP parsers with LLM agents to extract steroid dosing instructions from prescription signatures in OMOP CDM EHR databases. This work addresses a real gap: corticosteroid exposure is critical to pharmacoepidemiology, but dosing information is buried in free-text fields that structured fields don't capture.
whyItMatters: Medication dosing information in EHR data is largely inaccessible to computational research — structured fields capture what was ordered, not what was actually prescribed, titrated, or tapered. AgentDose (nominated for OHDSI 2025 Best Community Contribution) aims to make steroid exposure estimation reproducible and scalable across distributed OHDSI partner databases.

title: Health Equity in Clinical Data & Algorithms
description: I'm interested in how structural inequities in the healthcare system are encoded in — and sometimes amplified by — clinical data and the models trained on it. My work on racial disparities in post-hysterectomy emergency department visits (Int J Gynaecol Obstet, in-press) identified systematic differences in 30-day ED utilization across racial groups using a multi-state HCUP cohort of ~200,000 cases. I'm also contributing to work on SDoH data in suicide prediction models.
whyItMatters: Health equity is not a separate topic from methods — it is a lens that should be applied to every methodological choice: how cohorts are defined, what outcomes are measured, which confounders are adjusted for, and how model performance is reported. Disparities that are invisible in aggregate statistics can drive systematic harm at the level of individual patients.

title: Causal Inference & Predictive Modeling
description: I'm developing fluency in both predictive and causal modeling for clinical data — and in being explicit about which mode is appropriate for a given question. My work includes survival analysis, comparative effectiveness evaluation of immunosuppressant therapies, and RL-based decision-making for renal replacement therapy in ICU-acquired AKI. I'm also building toward causal approaches for understanding pregnancy complications in real-world healthcare data.
whyItMatters: Most clinical prediction models are correlational, but clinical decisions are inherently causal. The distinction matters enormously for deployment: a model that predicts well may guide the wrong intervention if the association it captures is confounded. I'm exploring how to make that distinction explicit — and how to reason carefully about when causal claims are warranted.
